title: Reading and Use of English – Part 2
description: For questions 9–16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only ONE word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: Ants are social insects that live in highly organized colonies, (0) where each individual performs a specific role for the benefit of the group. (9) Although an ant has a very simple brain, the collective intelligence of the colony can solve incredibly complex problems. (10) Such is the coordination of these insects that they can build massive structures and find the shortest paths to food sources. (11) Provided that a chemical trail is established by a scout, hundreds of other ants will quickly follow it to the target. (12) Not only do they farm other insects like aphids, but they also cultivate fungi in sophisticated underground gardens. (13) Regardless of the size of the colony, the division of labor ensures that every task, from foraging to nursing, is completed. (14) Not until scientists began using time-lapse photography did they realize how dynamic the internal life of a nest truly is. (15) Despite the lack of a central leader, the colony functions as a single 'superorganism' capable of adapting to changing environments. (16) Whether we can apply the principles of ant behavior to human logistics and robotics is an area of ongoing scientific research. These tiny creatures demonstrate the power of cooperation and shared goals.

example.text: Ants are social insects that live in highly organized colonies, (0) where each individual performs a specific role.
example.explanation: Relative adverb used to refer to a place or situation.

questions.explanation: Conjunction introducing a contrast or concession.
Determiner used for emphasis at the start of the clause.
Conjunction used to express a condition.
Part of the 'Not only... but also' structure.
Part of the fixed phrase 'Regardless of'.
Part of the 'Not until' structure triggering inversion ('did they realize').
Preposition used to introduce a contrast.
Conjunction introducing two possibilities or a doubt.
